item.name: data readiness levels
item.description: A proposed common language of banded levels (A, B, C with numbered sub-levels) for assessing and communicating the readiness of a data set for analysis.
item.grounding_span: In this position paper we introduce the idea of data readiness levels. Data readiness levels are designed to deal with a challenge for human cognitive information processing.

item.name: Band C
item.description: The band of data readiness concerned with the accessibility of a data set, including verification of existence, format, privacy, legal, ethical and topological constraints.
item.grounding_span: Band C is about the accessibility of a data set.

item.name: Band B
item.description: The band of data readiness concerned with the faithfulness and representation of the data once loaded into software: missing values, noise, entry errors, units, and collection-process characteristics.
item.grounding_span: Band B is about the faithfulness and representation of the data.

item.name: Band A
item.description: The band of data readiness concerned with data in context: the appropriateness of a data set to answer a particular question or support a particular analysis, requiring a defined task.
item.grounding_span: Band A is about data in context. It is at Band A that we consider the appropriateness of a given data set to answer a particular question or to be subject to a particular analysis.

item.name: hearsay data
item.description: Data whose existence is asserted but unverified; the lowest sub-level (C4) of Band C.
item.grounding_span: We might think of it as hearsay data. Data that you’ve heard about so you say it’s there.

item.name: C1
item.description: The top of Band C: data ready to load into analysis software or share, machine readable, with ethical procedures for data handling addressed.
item.grounding_span: When data arrives at C1 it’s ready to be loaded into analysis software, or it can be made available for others to access (e.g. via a data repository such as OpenML [3]).

item.name: B1
item.description: The top of Band B: the analyst has a broad idea of the limitations of the data and how faithful it is to its description.
item.grounding_span: By the end of Band B, when data is B1, a broad idea of limitations in the data should be present in the expert’s mind.

item.name: A1
item.description: The top of Band A: data considered alongside a defined task with remedial steps taken, ready to be deployed in the given context.
item.grounding_span: Once data has been considered alongside a task and any remedial steps have been taken, then the data is in A1 condition.

item.name: data quality
item.description: The quality of a data set, argued to require as much control and measurement as model quality.
item.grounding_span: We need to develop a lot more control over the quality of our data.

item.name: technology readiness levels
item.description: A numbered scale, originating at NASA, that makes the readiness of a technology manifest; the inspiration for data readiness levels.
item.grounding_span: Technology readiness levels arose in NASA [2]. The readiness of the technology is made manifest through a set of numbers which describe its characteristics

item.name: vivid information
item.description: Information weighted more heavily in reasoning than non-vivid (pallid) information; data must be rendered vivid to be accounted for in planning.
item.grounding_span: Psychologists refer to the idea of vivid information as information that is weighted more heavily in reasoning than non-vivid or pallid information.

item.name: availability heuristics
item.description: Cognitive heuristics potentially related to why abstract, pallid concepts like data are under-weighted in planning.
item.grounding_span: This may relate to availability heuristics[1].

item.name: data munging
item.description: The processing work of getting data into usable form, forming part (but not all) of Band C.
item.grounding_span: Some parts of Band C are sometimes referred to as “data munging” or “data wrangling”, but those aren’t the only components of this band

item.name: Exploratory Data Analysis
item.description: Tukey's approach of visualizing and exploring data, situated within Band B to render data vivid and involve decision makers.
item.grounding_span: Tukey’s approach of “Exploratory Data Analysis” also fits within Band B.

item.name: missing values
item.description: Absent data entries whose handling and encoding must be understood in Band B; mishandling before aggregation can invalidate sections of data.
item.grounding_span: How are missing values handled, what is their encoding?

item.name: data collection bias
item.description: Bias or non-randomization in the process by which data was collected, to be verified as part of Band B.
item.grounding_span: As part of Band B the characteristics of the collection process should also be verified, was data collection randomized, is it biased in any particular way?

item.name: task definition
item.description: The defined question or task against which a data set's appropriateness is judged; a prerequisite for Band A assessment.
item.grounding_span: A data set can only be considered in Band A once a task is defined.

item.name: data curation
item.description: The work of collating, processing and curating data, whose cost is systematically underestimated and under-resourced.
item.grounding_span: Costs of data curation are often underestimated and those who do the work in Band C and Band B are very often undervalued.

item.name: deanonymization
item.description: Re-identification risk arising when anonymized data is combined with related data sets.
item.grounding_span: medical data can be easily deanonymized if it is combined with other related data sets

item.name: combining data sets
item.description: Bringing two data sets together to form a new data set, which triggers fresh readiness assessment at Bands C and B.
item.grounding_span: What happens if we bring two data sets together to form a new data set?

item.name: meta data science
item.description: The study of data science itself through acquiring data about data science, required to quantitatively evaluate data readiness levels.
item.grounding_span: meta data science, i.e. the study of data science itself through acquiring data about data science

item.name: spreadsheet analysis errors
item.description: Common errors introduced by spreadsheet software, such as columns perturbed by partial sorts or gene names converted to dates, checked at Band B.
item.grounding_span: If the data has been through a spreadsheet software, can you confirm that no common spreadsheet analysis errors were made?

item.name: data agglomeration
item.description: Aggregation of data (e.g. for privacy) whose interaction with missing values can invalidate the data, as in the Uganda case study.
item.grounding_span: If the data has been agglomerated at some point (for example, for privacy) how were missing values dealt with before agglomeration?

item.name: ligature
item.description: A single typeset unit formed from letters running together; a source of character-encoding snafus in the PMLR data wrangling case study.
item.grounding_span: This meant that abstracts contained ligatures.

item.name: statistical design
item.description: Careful design of the analysis relative to the question, belonging in Band A where the question is known.
item.grounding_span: It is in Band A where you should be carefully thinking about the statistical design because it is only when you have the question you wish to answer that you can really unpick how your data may be biased or what information is missing.

item.name: data annotation
item.description: Expert human annotation of data, potentially required to bring a data set up to A1.
item.grounding_span: To bring a data set up to A1, there may be a need for significant annotation of the data by human expert.

item.name: machine learning
item.description: The field whose academic focus on model quality, rather than data quality, motivates the paper.
item.grounding_span: Much of the focus on machine learning in the academic world of machine learning is on the quality of the model.

item.name: Big Data
item.description: The era in which questions and data are badly characterized and data discussions in decision-making forums lack depth.
item.grounding_span: This is particularly true in the era of Big Data

item.name: project management
item.description: Planning, scoping and resourcing of data projects, which data readiness levels are proposed to facilitate.
item.grounding_span: speculates on how formalisation of these levels into a common language for data readiness could facilitate project management

item.name: noise characterization
item.description: Characterization of sensor noise or manual data entry errors, examined in Band B.
item.grounding_span: What is the noise characterization (for sensors) or for manual data are there data entry errors?

item.claim_text: Anyone performing an analysis at Band A needs to be intimately familiar with the collection process so that biases in data collection can be understood.

item.claim_text: A data set formed by combining two assessed data sets still requires its own data readiness assessment.

item.claim_text: Medical data can be easily deanonymized if it is combined with other related data sets.

item.claim_text: Bringing the PMLR website data from C4 to C1 took approximately six working days despite no privacy or intellectual property issues.
item.grounding_span: There were no issues around privacy or intellectual property but work still took approximately six working days.

item.claim_text: In the Uganda disease monitoring project, data collation took around 70-80% of the work.
item.grounding_span: The data collation in itself took probably around 70-80% of the work on the project.

item.claim_text: In the Uganda case study, a data processing error at Band B produced a negative result at Band A.
item.grounding_span: In other words, due to a data processing error at Band B, a negative result was obtained at Band A.

item.claim_text: There is no substitute for directly looking at the data.
item.grounding_span: There was no substitute for directly looking at the data.

item.claim_text: Many failings on large data projects may be associated with failure to provision resource for data preparation rather than failings in the algorithmics of the system.
item.grounding_span: it may be that many failings on large data projects are associated with a failure to provision resource for the challenges involved in preparing our data, rather than a failing in the algorithmics of the system

item.claim_text: Costs of data curation are often underestimated and those who do the work in Bands C and B are very often undervalued.

item.claim_text: Data readiness levels highlight the different skill sets required in each stage of analysis, from software engineer to data-munger to data scientist to machine learning scientist.

item.name: AI-ready data
item.description: Public statistical data prepared so that AI systems can discover, interpret, and relay it accurately; builds on open-data principles.
item.grounding_span: Creating AI-ready data builds on established open-data principles.

item.name: Generative AI
item.description: AI systems that generate answers for users and act as intermediaries in the consumption of public statistical information.
item.grounding_span: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is redefining how people interact with public information and shaping how public data are consumed.

item.name: Large language models
item.description: Language models powering AI chatbots and assistants that increasingly draw on public datasets.
item.grounding_span: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) mean that more Americans are getting answers from AI chatbots and other AI systems, which increasingly draw on public datasets.

item.name: Statistical APIs
item.description: Data access interfaces that, when enhanced with metadata and context, support accurate and trusted generative AI results.
item.grounding_span: Statistical Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) enhanced with metadata and context to support accurate and trusted generative AI results for large language models.

item.name: Metadata
item.description: Descriptive information about data (variables, units, update timestamps) that enables machines to interpret data correctly.
item.grounding_span: By enriching data access points with high-quality metadata and exposing them through standardized, AI-friendly APIs, agencies can significantly improve how LLMs discover, interpret, and relay publicly available federal statistics.

item.name: Variable-level metadata
item.description: Detailed metadata attached to each data element, recommended by the Commerce report for machine understandability.
item.grounding_span: metadata for machine understandability” – in other words, giving AI detailed context about each data element

item.name: Machine-readable data
item.description: Data published in formats machines can parse, as required of agencies by the OPEN Government Data Act.
item.grounding_span: requires agencies to publish data in machine-readable formats “while ensuring no semantic meaning is lost.”

item.name: Machine-understandable data
item.description: Data whose meaning, not just format, is accessible to machines; positioned as the step beyond machine-readable.
item.grounding_span: Today’s AI era demands federal agencies go further – from machine-readable to machine-understandable data.

item.name: Machine interpretability
item.description: The degree to which public data can be interpreted by machines; subject of a cross-agency landscape analysis under an NSDS project.
item.grounding_span: landscape analysis of “machine interpretability” of public data is already being explored through a National Secure Data Service (NSDS) project

item.name: Data quality
item.description: The quality of data elements assessed across the FCSM Framework's domains and dimensions.
item.grounding_span: Each data element should be assessed for data quality across these three domains and corresponding dimensions including accessibility, timeliness, and accuracy.

item.name: Data quality dimensions
item.description: Dimensions of the FCSM Framework that federal statistics used in AI-driven interactions should meet.
item.grounding_span: meet the data quality dimensions of the Framework including, but not limited to, accessibility, timeliness, accuracy, and credibility

item.name: Data quality domains
item.description: The three FCSM Framework domains used to evaluate metadata improvements.
item.grounding_span: The FCSM framework provides three domains to evaluate these metadata improvements: Utility, Objectivity, and Integrity.

item.name: Fitness for use
item.description: The suitability of a data set for a purpose, which the FCSM Framework for Data Quality helps analysts and the public assess.
item.grounding_span: developed the Framework for Data Quality to help analysts and the public assess fitness for use of data sets

item.name: Federal statistics
item.description: Statistical data produced by federal agencies, which should be front-and-center in AI-driven experiences.
item.grounding_span: The federal statistical community can take action to advance the use of federal statistics with generative AI to ensure that official statistics are front-and-center

item.name: AI chatbots
item.description: Conversational AI systems from which more Americans are getting answers, drawing on public datasets.
item.grounding_span: more Americans are getting answers from AI chatbots and other AI systems, which increasingly draw on public datasets

item.name: Open-data principles
item.description: Established principles for publishing public data openly, on which AI-ready data builds.
item.grounding_span: Creating AI-ready data builds on established open-data principles.

item.name: Machine consumption
item.description: Format-oriented optimization of data elements and APIs for consumption by machines, contrasted with conveying meaning.
item.grounding_span: ensuring data elements and AI-friendly APIs are optimized for machine consumption

item.name: Authoritative data
item.description: Official, authoritative government data whose prioritization in AI tools over nonauthoritative sources the Commerce guidance seeks to increase.
item.grounding_span: increase the prioritization of its authoritative data in AI tools over nonauthoritative sources

item.name: AI-optimized data structures
item.description: Data structures designed for easy parsing by AI, such as simplified JSON responses.

item.name: AI-readiness
item.description: The extent to which a data asset is prepared for effective analysis and querying by AI systems, particularly LLMs.
item.grounding_span: For the purposes of this project, “AI-readiness” refers to the extent to which a data asset is prepared for effective analysis and querying by AI systems, particularly LLMs.

item.name: Large language model
item.description: AI models whose ability to accurately respond to questions about federal statistical data is the object of the proposed evaluation.
item.grounding_span: This Request for Solutions (RFS) seeks to develop an empirical evaluation that measures the ability of large language models (LLMs) to accurately respond to questions that require an understanding of federal statistical open Government data assets and their associated metadata.

item.name: Generative AI
item.description: AI applications enabling natural-language and multimodal interaction with public data.
item.grounding_span: Generative AI applications offer transformative opportunities for how Americans interact with public data.

item.name: Federal statistical data assets
item.description: The portfolio of statistical products and data assets published by federal statistical agencies.
item.grounding_span: the creation of prompt-response pairs that address a representative sample of the complex portfolio of federal statistical products and data assets

item.name: Open government data assets
item.description: Data assets defined in statute and OMB guidance, including datasets and their accompanying contextual information.
item.grounding_span: Open government data assets are defined in statute (44 USC 3502(20)) and in M-25-05.

item.name: Metadata
item.description: Descriptive information about data assets that enables usage, management, cataloging, discovery, and interoperability.
item.grounding_span: requires federal agencies to capture and store rich metadata about their data assets to enable effective internal usage, management, data cataloging, discovery, and interoperability

item.name: Documentation
item.description: Accompanying contextual information for datasets that can influence LLM interpretation.
item.grounding_span: Solutions should consider both datasets and their accompanying contextual information (e.g., metadata, documentation, and formatting) as this information can influence how an LLM interprets the dataset.

item.name: Prompt-response pairs
item.description: Curated question-answer pairs used to assess LLM accuracy, relevancy, and explainability in federal statistical use cases.
item.grounding_span: This will involve the creation of prompt-response pairs necessary to assess the accuracy, relevancy, and explainability of LLMs in federal statistical use cases.

item.name: National Secure Data Service
item.description: A future federal service envisioned to host the evaluation tool as a shared service.
item.grounding_span: this RFS envisions the development of a tool that may be offered as part of a shared service within a future National Secure Data Service (NSDS)

item.name: Shared service
item.description: A service-delivery model under which the evaluation tool would be offered within a future NSDS.
item.grounding_span: a tool that may be offered as part of a shared service within a future National Secure Data Service (NSDS)

item.name: Statistical accuracy
item.description: The standard of accuracy an LLM must meet when interpreting and using statistical data assets.
item.grounding_span: develop an evaluation that can be used to assess an LLM’s ability to meet a high bar of statistical accuracy

item.name: Accuracy of LLM responses
item.description: A quality dimension of LLM responses assessed via prompt-response pairs.
item.grounding_span: prompt-response pairs necessary to assess the accuracy, relevancy, and explainability of LLMs in federal statistical use cases

item.name: Relevancy of LLM responses
item.description: A quality dimension of LLM responses assessed via prompt-response pairs.
item.grounding_span: prompt-response pairs necessary to assess the accuracy, relevancy, and explainability of LLMs in federal statistical use cases

item.name: Explainability of LLM responses
item.description: A quality dimension of LLM responses assessed via prompt-response pairs.
item.grounding_span: prompt-response pairs necessary to assess the accuracy, relevancy, and explainability of LLMs in federal statistical use cases

item.name: Machine-readability
item.description: A property of data assets identified as an improvement opportunity for LLM interaction.
item.grounding_span: highlighting opportunities to improve metadata quality, accessibility, and machine-readability

item.name: Metadata quality
item.description: A potential barrier to effective LLM interaction with statistical data assets.
item.grounding_span: identifying potential barriers related to metadata quality, accessibility, and data formatting

item.name: Accessibility
item.description: The ease of access to data assets, identified as an improvement opportunity and potential barrier.
item.grounding_span: highlighting opportunities to improve metadata quality, accessibility, and machine-readability

item.name: Data formatting
item.description: How data are formatted, identified as a potential barrier to LLM interaction.
item.grounding_span: identifying potential barriers related to metadata quality, accessibility, and data formatting

item.name: Data quality
item.description: A component of AI-readiness as defined for this project.
item.grounding_span: This includes data quality, access, formatting, and metadata.

item.name: Chat-based interfaces
item.description: Natural-language and multimodal interaction modes through which generative AI provides access to data collections.
item.grounding_span: By enabling interaction through natural language and multimodal prompts, these technologies facilitate more intuitive access to complex data collections through chat-based interfaces, reducing technical barriers and expanding the accessibility of public data to a broader range of users.

item.name: Data cataloging
item.description: A metadata-enabled data management function named in the Evidence Act Phase 2 guidance.
item.grounding_span: to enable effective internal usage, management, data cataloging, discovery, and interoperability

item.name: Data discovery
item.description: A metadata-enabled function named in the Evidence Act Phase 2 guidance.
item.grounding_span: to enable effective internal usage, management, data cataloging, discovery, and interoperability

item.name: Interoperability
item.description: The ability of systems to work together, targeted by metadata requirements and emerging standards such as the Model Context Protocol.
item.grounding_span: They are also assessing the use of emerging standards like the Model Context Protocol to increase system interoperability.

item.name: Subject-matter expertise
item.description: Domain expertise used to inform prompt-response pair development and as a comparison baseline for LLM responses.
item.grounding_span: Leveraging subject-matter expertise to inform the development of prompt-response pairs, ensuring that the evaluation reflects real-world analytical demands and domain relevance.

item.name: Data stewards
item.description: Personnel engaged alongside subject-matter experts to create prompt-response pairs.
item.grounding_span: to create a collection of prompt-response pairs through engagement with subject-matter experts and data stewards (e.g., RFI, facilitated workshop)

item.name: Performance metrics
item.description: Quantifiable measures of LLM response quality against evaluation prompts.
item.grounding_span: Establishing quantifiable performance metrics to evaluate the quality of LLM responses when engaging with federal statistical data.

item.name: Open-source software
item.description: The licensing and development approach required for the evaluation tool and project code.
item.grounding_span: All technical components should propose using open-source software and open-source software development principles wherever feasible.

item.name: AI-readiness criteria
item.description: Criteria being developed under the NSDS demonstration project AI-RD-24 to assess and improve AI compatibility of federal statistical data.
item.grounding_span: aims to assess and improve the AI compatibility of federal statistical data by developing AI-readiness criteria and prototyping tools

item.name: API enrichment with metadata and context
item.description: The FCSM-recommended practice of adding metadata and context to APIs to improve LLM results.
item.grounding_span: a call to action to improve the AI-readiness of federal statistical data by enhancing APIs with metadata and context to improve LLM results

item.name: Publication standards
item.description: The standards under which statistical data assets were published, hypothesized to affect LLM interaction differently by vintage.
item.grounding_span: The pilot should include both older and more recently published statistical data assets to determine whether older publication standards create different challenges for LLM interaction compared to newer standards.

item.name: Structured and unstructured data formats
item.description: The two broad data format classes from which AI-ready data enable LLMs to generate insights.
item.grounding_span: AI-ready data enable LLMs to generate meaningful insights from both structured (e.g., tabular) and unstructured (e.g., reports) formats by facilitating accurate interpretation and contextual understanding.

item.name: Evidence-based and data-driven decision-making
item.description: The decision-making mode the evaluation tool is intended to facilitate during the DCAT-US 3.0 transition.
item.grounding_span: This will facilitate evidence-based and data-driven decision-making as the government transitions to DCAT-US 3.0 and takes the necessary steps to make its data AI-ready.

item.verbatim_text: For the purposes of this project, “AI-readiness” refers to the extent to which a data asset is prepared for effective analysis and querying by AI systems, particularly LLMs. This includes data quality, access, formatting, and metadata.

item.claim_text: Generative AI applications offer transformative opportunities for how Americans interact with public data.
item.grounding_span: Generative AI applications offer transformative opportunities for how Americans interact with public data.

item.claim_text: Natural-language and multimodal chat interfaces make complex data collections more accessible by reducing technical barriers.
item.grounding_span: By enabling interaction through natural language and multimodal prompts, these technologies facilitate more intuitive access to complex data collections through chat-based interfaces, reducing technical barriers and expanding the accessibility of public data to a broader range of users.

item.claim_text: The federal government must optimize and enrich its data assets with appropriate context to keep federal data valuable for generative AI training.
item.grounding_span: To ensure that federal data are increasingly valuable in the training of generative AI applications, the federal government must optimize and enrich its data assets with the appropriate context for this rapidly evolving ecosystem.

item.claim_text: The federal government has made great strides in developing best practices for AI-ready data.
item.grounding_span: The federal government has made great strides in developing best practices for AI-ready data

item.claim_text: Contextual information (metadata, documentation, formatting) can influence how an LLM interprets a dataset.
item.grounding_span: Solutions should consider both datasets and their accompanying contextual information (e.g., metadata, documentation, and formatting) as this information can influence how an LLM interprets the dataset.

item.claim_text: AI-ready data enable LLMs to generate meaningful insights from both structured and unstructured formats.
item.grounding_span: AI-ready data enable LLMs to generate meaningful insights from both structured (e.g., tabular) and unstructured (e.g., reports) formats by facilitating accurate interpretation and contextual understanding.

item.claim_text: The Evidence Act Phase 2 implementation guidance requires federal agencies to capture and store rich metadata about their data assets.
item.grounding_span: This guidance incorporates a critical element of the Phase 2 Implementation of the Foundations for Evidence-Based Policymaking Act of 2018: Open Government Data Access and Management Guidance that requires federal agencies to capture and store rich metadata about their data assets to enable effective internal usage, management, data cataloging, discovery, and interoperability.

item.claim_text: The guidance recommends federal agencies update their metadata to comply with the DCAT-US 3.0 standard.
item.grounding_span: Specifically, it recommends federal agencies update their metadata to comply with the DCAT-US 3.0 standard.

item.claim_text: A tool for repeated evaluation of LLM responses will enable agencies to investigate whether enriching data, metadata, and documentation improves LLM response accuracy and relevancy.
item.grounding_span: Developing a tool that can conduct repeated evaluations of how well LLM responses interpret and use federal statistical data will enable agencies to investigate the effectiveness of enriching their data, metadata, and documentation in improving the accuracy and relevancy of LLM responses in federal statistical use cases.

item.claim_text: The FCSM has published a call to action to improve AI-readiness of federal statistical data by enhancing APIs with metadata and context.
item.grounding_span: Furthermore, the Federal Committee on Statistical Methodology (FCSM) has published a call to action to improve the AI-readiness of federal statistical data by enhancing APIs with metadata and context to improve LLM results.

item.claim_text: The FCSM is assessing emerging standards such as the Model Context Protocol to increase system interoperability.
item.grounding_span: They are also assessing the use of emerging standards like the Model Context Protocol to increase system interoperability.

item.claim_text: The important next step beyond existing AI-readiness initiatives is assessing whether statistical data are accurately captured and represented in LLM responses.
item.grounding_span: While these initiatives provide a strong foundation for improving AI-readiness, an important next step is to assess whether statistical data are being accurately captured and represented in responses generated by LLMs.

item.claim_text: The Commerce generative AI guidance, though designed for Commerce, has been made publicly available for open data publishers globally.
item.grounding_span: While designed for Commerce’s use, the guidance has been made publicly available to empower open data publishers across the globe to optimize their data for generative AI systems.

item.claim_text: BEA, the Census Bureau, NCSES, and other federal statistical agencies have played a major role in the AI-RD-24 project.

item.name: Data Readiness for AI
item.description: The suitability and readiness of a dataset for use in AI applications, a concept the paper says lacks a standard definition and is still evolving.
item.grounding_span: The concept of “Data Readiness for AI” lacks a standard definition and is still evolving.

item.name: Data quality
item.description: Traditional data quality assessment dimensions such as completeness, accuracy, consistency, and reliability, evaluated by existing toolkits.
item.grounding_span: Many tools concentrate on specific aspects of data quality ([17, 30, 46, 47]), while others emphasize some factors of data readiness ([5, 25, 29]).

item.name: Completeness
item.description: Presence of all required data and attribute values within a dataset; a Quality-category readiness dimension.
item.grounding_span: Completeness: Measures the presence of all required data.

item.name: Outliers
item.description: Anomalous data points that deviate from the norm; a Quality-category readiness dimension.
item.grounding_span: Outliers: Identifies anomalous data points that deviate from the norm.

item.name: Duplication
item.description: Presence of duplicate or redundant records in a dataset; a Quality-category readiness dimension.
item.grounding_span: Duplication: Evaluates the presence of duplicate records.

item.name: Data preparation practices
item.description: Robustness of the methods used to prepare data; a Quality-category readiness dimension.
item.grounding_span: Data preparation practices: Assesses the robustness of the methods used to prepare the data.

item.name: Timeliness
item.description: Whether data is up-to-date and relevant; a Quality-category readiness dimension.
item.grounding_span: Timeliness: Ensures data is up-to-date and relevant.

item.name: Understandability
item.description: Readiness category covering metadata availability and quality, provenance, and user interfaces for data access; assessed in AIDRIN via FAIR principle compliance.
item.grounding_span: Among the categories described in Section 3, we provide Quality, Understandability (using FAIR principle compliance), Value, Fairness, and Bias metrics in AIDRIN.

item.name: Metadata availability and quality
item.description: Whether comprehensive metadata is present to describe the dataset; an Understandability-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Metadata availability and quality: Ensures comprehensive metadata is present to describe the dataset.

item.name: Provenance
item.description: Origin and lineage of the data; an Understandability-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Provenance: Tracks the origin and lineage of the data, ensuring accuracy and completeness.

item.name: User interfaces for data access
item.description: Ease of accessing and interacting with the data; an Understandability-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: User interfaces for data access: Evaluates the ease of accessing and interacting with the data.

item.name: Structural quality
item.description: Readiness category covering data types, schema quality, file formats/storage systems, and data access performance; still in development in AIDRIN.
item.grounding_span: While the privacy aspect of Governance is already integrated into AIDRIN, other components addressing Governance and Structural Quality are currently in development.

item.name: Used data types
item.description: Appropriateness and consistency of data types; a Structural-quality dimension.
item.grounding_span: Used data types: Assesses the appropriateness and consistency of data types.

item.name: Quality of data schema
item.description: Design and structure of the data schema supporting normalized forms and fast storage/access; a Structural-quality dimension.
item.grounding_span: Quality of data schema: Evaluates the design and structure of the data schema that supports normalized forms and fast data storage and access.

item.name: File format and data storage system
item.description: Efficiency and suitability of file formats and storage systems; a Structural-quality dimension.
item.grounding_span: File format and used data storage system: Reviews the efficiency and suitability of file formats and storage systems.

item.name: Data access performance
item.description: Speed and reliability of data retrieval; a Structural-quality dimension.
item.grounding_span: Data access performance: Measures the speed and reliability of data retrieval.

item.name: Value (of data)
item.description: Readiness category covering feature importance, labels, data point impact, and uncertainty in data.
item.grounding_span: Among the categories described in Section 3, we provide Quality, Understandability (using FAIR principle compliance), Value, Fairness, and Bias metrics in AIDRIN.

item.name: Feature importance
item.description: Significance of different features within the dataset for a prediction task; a Value-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Feature importance: Assesses the significance of different features within the dataset.

item.name: Labels
item.description: Availability, quality, and correctness of labels for supervised learning; a Value-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Labels: Examines the availability, quality, and correctness of labels for supervised learning.

item.name: Data point impact
item.description: Influence of individual data points on the overall dataset; a Value-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Data point impact: Evaluates the influence of individual data points on the overall dataset.

item.name: Uncertainty in data
item.description: Uncertainty or confidence in the data, measured via uncertainty quantification methods; a Value-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Uncertainty in data: Measures uncertainty or confidence in the data using uncertainty quantification methods.

item.name: Fairness
item.description: Equal treatment of different groups or individuals in a dataset, particularly with respect to sensitive attributes.
item.grounding_span: Fairness in a dataset refers to the equal treatment of different groups or individuals, particularly concerning sensitive attributes such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status.

item.name: Bias
item.description: Unfair biases in datasets that can lead to discriminatory outcomes when ML models are trained on them.
item.grounding_span: According to Mehrabi et al. [36] unfair biases in datasets can lead to discriminatory outcomes when machine learning models are trained on them, potentially extending and increasing existing societal inequalities.

item.name: Class imbalance
item.description: Disparity in the distribution of classes within a dataset; a Fairness-and-bias-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Class imbalance: Assesses the distribution of classes within the dataset.

item.name: Class separability
item.description: How well different classes can be distinguished; a Fairness-and-bias-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Class separability: Measures how well different classes can be distinguished.

item.name: Discrimination index
item.description: Identification of potential biases in the data; a Fairness-and-bias-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Discrimination index: Identifies potential biases in the data.

item.name: Population representation
item.description: Diverse and representative sampling of the population; a Fairness-and-bias-category dimension.
item.grounding_span: Population representation: Ensures diverse and representative sampling of the population.

item.name: Governance
item.description: Readiness category covering collection, processing and curation, application, security, and privacy of data.
item.grounding_span: While the privacy aspect of Governance is already integrated into AIDRIN, other components addressing Governance and Structural Quality are currently in development.

item.name: Data collection governance
item.description: Consent, sampling methods, ethical considerations, regulatory compliance, and funding sources in data collection; a Governance dimension.
item.grounding_span: Collection: Reviews consent, sampling methods, ethical considerations, regulatory compliance, and funding sources.

item.name: Processing and curation
item.description: Anonymization, curation, and de-identification methods used on data; a Governance dimension.
item.grounding_span: Processing and curation: Assesses anonymization, curation, and de-identification methods used.

item.name: Application (usage restrictions)
item.description: Usage restrictions and potential biases in data analysis; a Governance dimension.
item.grounding_span: Application: Evaluates usage restrictions and potential biases in data analysis.

item.name: Security
item.description: Data sensitivity, access control mechanisms, and sharing protocols; a Governance dimension.
item.grounding_span: Security: Reviews data sensitivity, access control mechanisms, and sharing protocols.

item.name: Privacy
item.description: Privacy requirements, budgets, and scores for data; a Governance dimension assessed in AIDRIN via re-identification risk.
item.grounding_span: Privacy: Assesses privacy requirements, budgets, and scores.

item.name: Re-identification risk
item.description: Risk that personally identifiable information in a dataset can be re-identified.
item.grounding_span: It aims to assess the risk of re-identification of personally identifiable information in datasets.

item.name: AI application-specific metrics
item.description: User-defined metrics specific to the AI model being developed and its intended application.
item.grounding_span: Model-specific metrics: Evaluates metrics specific to the AI models and their intended applications, ensuring the data meets the requirements for successful model training and deployment.

item.name: FAIR principle compliance
item.description: Degree to which metadata complies with the Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable principles.
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN assesses the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) compliance of a given set of metadata.

item.name: Feature correlations
item.description: Direction and strength of association between variables/features in a dataset.
item.grounding_span: Correlation analysis is a fundamental statistical technique used to measure the direction and strength of the correlation between two quantitative variables.

item.name: Group fairness
item.description: Fairness across groups defined by sensitive attributes, measured in AIDRIN via representation rate and statistical rate.
item.grounding_span: In the context of AIDRIN, the representation rate and statistical rate emerge as effective metrics for measuring group fairness.

item.name: Federated learning
item.description: Distributed learning setting where evaluating the readiness of edge clients' data is important; an application area for AIDRIN.
item.grounding_span: Moreover, AIDRIN’s relevance extends into emerging areas like Federated Learning.

item.name: Shapley values
item.description: Game-theoretic technique for quantifying the impact of each feature on a model's predictions, used by AIDRIN for feature importance.
item.grounding_span: Shapley values provide a means to quantify the impact of each feature on a model’s predictions.

item.name: Aggregate AI readiness score
item.description: A planned single aggregate rating summarizing a dataset's readiness for AI, not yet defined because standards are still evolving.
item.grounding_span: Our goal is to aggregate a list of these metrics to develop an “AIDRIN Score” that summarizes the readiness of a given dataset for AI.

item.verbatim_text: Completeness signifies the existence of necessary data and attribute values within a dataset, indicating the extent to which data points or entries fully exist with relevant attribute values.
item.grounding_span: Completeness signifies the existence of necessary data and attribute values within a dataset, indicating the extent to which data points or entries fully exist with relevant attribute values.

item.verbatim_text: An outlier in a dataset denotes a data point or instance that deviates significantly from the anticipated values within the dataset.
item.grounding_span: An outlier in a dataset denotes a data point or instance that deviates significantly from the anticipated values within the dataset.

item.verbatim_text: Duplicates in a dataset refer to the existence of duplicate or redundant instances, potentially distorting the AI modeling process by introducing repetitive data entries.
item.grounding_span: Duplicates in a dataset refer to the existence of duplicate or redundant instances, potentially distorting the AI modeling process by introducing repetitive data entries.

item.verbatim_text: A privacy measure is a metric or method used to quantify the level of privacy protection in a given context, especially in the handling and release of sensitive data.
item.grounding_span: A privacy measure is a metric or method used to quantify the level of privacy protection in a given context, especially in the handling and release of sensitive data.

item.verbatim_text: Correlation analysis is a fundamental statistical technique used to measure the direction and strength of the correlation between two quantitative variables.
item.grounding_span: Correlation analysis is a fundamental statistical technique used to measure the direction and strength of the correlation between two quantitative variables.

item.verbatim_text: Fairness in a dataset refers to the equal treatment of different groups or individuals, particularly concerning sensitive attributes such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status.
item.grounding_span: Fairness in a dataset refers to the equal treatment of different groups or individuals, particularly concerning sensitive attributes such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status.

item.verbatim_text: Class imbalance in a dataset occurs when the number of instances in one class significantly differs from the number of instances in another class, particularly common in binary classification problems.
item.grounding_span: Class imbalance in a dataset occurs when the number of instances in one class significantly differs from the number of instances in another class, particularly common in binary classification problems.

item.verbatim_text: Timeliness: Ensures data is up-to-date and relevant.
item.grounding_span: Timeliness: Ensures data is up-to-date and relevant.

item.verbatim_text: Provenance: Tracks the origin and lineage of the data, ensuring accuracy and completeness.
item.grounding_span: Provenance: Tracks the origin and lineage of the data, ensuring accuracy and completeness.

item.claim_text: There are no standard methods or frameworks for assessing the readiness of data for AI.
item.grounding_span: However, there are no standard methods or frameworks for assessing the “readiness” of data for AI.

item.claim_text: The concept of Data Readiness for AI lacks a standard definition and is still evolving.
item.grounding_span: The concept of “Data Readiness for AI” lacks a standard definition and is still evolving.

item.claim_text: Models trained on low-quality, biased data are often ineffective.
item.grounding_span: As data is the fuel for AI, models trained on low-quality, biased data are often ineffective.

item.claim_text: The IQR method is more reliable for detecting outliers than the LOF method because it is less affected by extreme values.
item.grounding_span: The IQR method is more reliable for detecting outliers because it is less affected by extreme values than the LOF method.

item.claim_text: Theil's U is preferred over Cramer's V for categorical correlations because it handles unequal distributions and considers conditional probabilities.
item.grounding_span: Theil’s U is preferred over Cramer’s V for its ability to handle unequal distributions and its consideration of conditional probabilities.

item.claim_text: Unfair biases in datasets can lead to discriminatory outcomes when machine learning models are trained on them, potentially amplifying societal inequalities.
item.grounding_span: According to Mehrabi et al. [36] unfair biases in datasets can lead to discriminatory outcomes when machine learning models are trained on them, potentially extending and increasing existing societal inequalities.

item.claim_text: Class imbalance can lead machine learning models to discriminate their predictions against the minority class.
item.grounding_span: Hassanin et al. [26] highlighted that class imbalance can lead machine learning models to discriminate their predictions against the minority class.

item.claim_text: Many traditional unified statistical rates are limited to binary sensitive attributes and leave non-binary attributes unaddressed.
item.grounding_span: However, many traditional unified statistical rates are limited to binary-sensitive attributes, leaving non-binary attributes unaddressed.

item.claim_text: The TSD metric may not capture contextual factors and proxies of the sensitive attribute and cannot consider multiple sensitive attributes simultaneously.
item.grounding_span: However, the TSD metric may not capture contextual factors and proxies of the sensitive attribute within the dataset. It also cannot consider multiple sensitive attributes simultaneously, limiting its applicability.

item.claim_text: The computation of Shapley values was the most time-intensive aspect of AIDRIN in the performance evaluation.
item.grounding_span: In our performance evaluation, the most time-intensive aspect of AIDRIN was identified as the computation of Shapley values to evaluate crucial features within a dataset.

item.claim_text: AIDRIN's result-generation time increases from 0.8s to 5s as dataset size grows from 782 records to 1.5 million records.
item.grounding_span: As shown in the performance evaluations detailed in Table 3, it becomes evident that the time required to generate results gradually increases from 0.8s to 5s as the dataset size increases from 782 records to 1.5 million records.

item.claim_text: The SGC dataset's imbalance degree score of 4.49 indicates significant class distribution disparity.
item.grounding_span: The imbalance degree score of 4.49 indicates significant class distribution disparity.

item.claim_text: AIDRIN calculated a mean re-identification risk score of 0.45 for the SGC dataset's Housing feature, suggesting a potential re-identification risk.
item.grounding_span: When considering the privacy risk scores associated with the ‘Housing’ feature, AIDRIN calculated a mean value of the re-identification risk scores to be 0.45.

item.claim_text: The overall completeness of the TCGA-LUAD clinical dataset was 56%, with the lowest attribute completeness at 22%.
item.grounding_span: We observed that the overall completeness of the data was 56% with attributes having varying degrees of completeness scores, the lowest being 22%.

item.claim_text: In the TCGA-LUAD dataset, the TSD score across racial groups (0.15) versus gender groups (0.01) reflects potential discrimination in class attributes between racial groups compared to gender groups.
item.grounding_span: The scores reflect potential discrimination in class attributes between racial groups compared to gender groups.

item.claim_text: AIDRIN's current functionality is focused on tabular data, limiting its applicability to other data modalities.
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN’s current functionality is focused on tabular data, presenting a limitation in its applicability to other data modalities.

item.name: AIDRIN (AI Data Readiness Inspector)
item.owner: Hiniduma, Byna, Bez, and Madduri (Ohio State University, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Argonne National Laboratory)
item.method: Quantitative and qualitative assessment of datasets via readiness metrics (completeness, outliers, duplicates, privacy, correlations, fairness, FAIR compliance, class imbalance, feature importance) with visualizations and JSON reports, delivered through a Flask web interface and a PyPI package.
item.grounding_span: To provide a quantifiable assessment of the readiness of data for AI processes, we define parameters of data readiness for AI and introduce AIDRIN (AI Data Readiness INspector).

item.name: AI Fairness 360 (AIF360)
item.method: Open-source toolkit with bias detection metrics such as representation and statistical rates of sensitive attributes.
item.grounding_span: One of the tools addressing this aspect is IBM’s AI Fairness 360 toolkit [8] (AIF360), an open-source software designed to overcome biases in ML models.

item.name: Deequ
item.method: Library for defining unit tests for data, verifying explicit data quality checks on samples of large datasets.
item.grounding_span: Deequ [46] is another data quality assessing library designed for defining “unit tests for data,” enabling the measurement of data quality in large datasets.

item.name: DQLearn
item.method: Framework for organized quality operations: detecting, correcting, rule-based assessing, and explaining data quality issues.
item.grounding_span: Similarly, DQLearn [47] stands out as a framework designed for organized quality operations.

item.name: Informatica Data Quality Tool
item.method: Data profiling, cleansing, and monitoring with metrics for completeness, accuracy, consistency, and reliability.
item.grounding_span: Within the area of data quality toolkits, Informatica’s Data Quality Tool [30] is an open-source solution designed for data profiling, cleansing, and monitoring capabilities.

item.name: Data Readiness Report
item.method: Generates documentation of data quality and readiness across standardized dimensions, documenting properties, quality issues, and data operations.
item.grounding_span: Data Readiness Report [5] focuses on addressing challenges in data preprocessing within machine learning pipelines.

item.name: Data Quality Toolkit (Gupta et al.)
item.method: Automated explanations across data quality dimensions such as metadata, provenance, simple statistics, and linear correlations.
item.grounding_span: Gupta et al. [25] designed a toolkit to provide automated explanations across various dimensions of data quality, such as metadata data information, data provenance, simple statistics of data features, and linear correlations between features to simplify data preparation and improve the quality of training data for AI.

item.name: ESS-DIVE FAIR assessment
item.method: Quantitative FAIR compliance assessment of published earth-science data using metrics such as search performance, metadata completeness, and user feedback.
item.grounding_span: ESS-DIVE [18] is a data repository for earth sciences data, which evaluates the FAIR principle compliance for the data being published.

item.text: Proportion of missing values within each feature of the dataset (Blake et al. completeness metric).
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN quantifies this by measuring the proportion of missing values within each feature of the dataset.

item.text: IQR-based outlier score quantifying the proportion of outliers in each feature.
item.grounding_span: The resulting outlier score, ranging from 0 to 1, quantifies the proportion of outliers in each feature of the dataset, providing a robust assessment of data variability.

item.text: Uniqueness-based duplication score comparing the number of unique items to the total number of items.
item.grounding_span: By comparing the number of unique items to the total number of items, it generates a single score indicating the level of duplication throughout the dataset.

item.text: MM risk score: Markov-model-based quantification of re-identification risk.
item.grounding_span: The authors propose the “MM risk score”, which uses the Markov model to quantify re-identification risks.

item.text: Pearson correlation coefficient for numerical feature correlations.
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN utilizes the Pearson correlation coefficient to gauge the strength and direction of numerical feature correlations.

item.text: Theil's U for categorical feature correlations, an asymmetric information-theoretic association measure.
item.grounding_span: On the other hand, for the assessment of categorical feature correlations, AIDRIN uses Theil’s U.

item.text: Representation rate: distribution of different sensitive attributes in the dataset.
item.grounding_span: The representation rate assesses the distributions of different sensitive attributes in the dataset.

item.text: Statistical rate: fairness evaluated through conditional probabilities of class labels given sensitive attribute values.
item.grounding_span: The statistical rate, on the other hand, evaluates fairness through conditional probabilities, ensuring that the target representations do not discriminate certain groups based on sensitive attributes.

item.text: Target Standard Deviation (TSD): standard deviation of target probabilities across all subgroups of a sensitive attribute, extending fairness measurement beyond binary groups.
item.grounding_span: To tackle this limitation, we introduce the Target Standard Deviation (TSD) metric.

item.text: FAIR compliance score: proportion of fulfilled metadata checks out of total possible checks across Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable categories.
item.grounding_span: The overall FAIR compliance score is presented as a percentage, reflecting the proportion of fulfilled checks out of the total possible checks.

item.text: Imbalance Degree (ID): class imbalance measure considering specific characteristics of the class distribution; AIDRIN's chosen imbalance metric.
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN uses the ID as the chosen metric to measure class imbalance.

item.text: Imbalance Ratio (IR): numerical representation of the disparity between majority and minority class instances.
item.grounding_span: One such metric is the Imbalance Ratio (IR), introduced by Francisco et al. [6].

item.text: Likelihood Ratio Imbalance Degree (LRID): imbalance measure comparing the existing class distribution to a balanced distribution via the likelihood ratio test.
item.grounding_span: In response to these challenges, the Likelihood Ratio Imbalance Degree (LRID), introduced by Zhu et al. [51], emerges as a novel metric.

item.text: Shapley values computed from a trained Random Forest model to quantify each feature's contribution to the prediction task.
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN uses Shapley values [33] as a method to measure feature importance

item.name: DCAT-US Schema (Project Open Data Metadata Schema)
item.grounding_span: AIDRIN currently supports the assessment of FAIR compliance of metadata using two metadata standards: DCAT [3] and Datacite [2].

item.name: DataCite Metadata Schema
item.grounding_span: Similarly, AIDRIN supports corresponding FAIR elements in the DataCite standard.

item.name: FAIR principles
item.grounding_span: FAIR principles [23] have emerged to guide data publishers to improve the quality of data.

item.name: Categories of Data Readiness for AI (seven-category classification)
item.grounding_span: In our effort to design a comprehensive framework and metrics for AI readiness, we propose seven categories of evaluation and several metrics in each category.

item.name: APPFL (Advanced Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning)

item.name: Data Nutrition Label

item.name: AI readiness
item.description: An organization's preparedness to adopt and leverage AI, measured by the Index across six pillars; framed as ongoing work rather than a one-time achievement.
item.grounding_span: The Index measures AI readiness of companies across six pillars: Strategy, Infrastructure, Data, Governance, Talent, and Culture.

item.name: Pacesetters
item.description: The top readiness category: fully prepared organizations (about 13-14% of those surveyed) that outperform peers on every measure of AI value.
item.grounding_span: Pacesetters (fully prepared)

item.name: Chasers
item.description: The second readiness category: moderately prepared organizations.
item.grounding_span: Chasers (moderately prepared)

item.name: Followers
item.description: The third readiness category: organizations with limited preparedness.
item.grounding_span: Followers (limited preparedness)

item.name: Laggards
item.description: The lowest readiness category: unprepared organizations.
item.grounding_span: Laggards (unprepared)

item.name: AI Infrastructure Debt
item.description: A concept introduced by the report: the accumulation of gaps, trade-offs, shortcuts and lags in compute, networking, data management, security, and talent that compound as companies rush to deploy AI.
item.grounding_span: AI Infrastructure Debt is the accumulation of gaps, trade-offs, short-cuts and lags in compute, networking, data management, security, and talent that compound as companies rush to deploy AI.

item.name: Technical debt
item.description: The software-world predecessor of AI Infrastructure Debt: code written quickly to meet deadlines that eventually slows innovation.
item.grounding_span: In the software world, this became known as technical debt: code written quickly to meet deadlines that eventually slows innovation.

item.name: Digital debt
item.description: A prior form of accumulated transformation-era compromises, named alongside technical debt as the lineage of AI Infrastructure Debt.
item.grounding_span: the modern evolution of technical and digital debt that held back prior transformation efforts

item.name: Agentic AI
item.description: AI systems that go beyond providing answers to take action autonomously across tasks, workflows, and business decisions.
item.grounding_span: Agentic AI systems don’t simply provide answers; as shown in the chart below, they take action by navigating tasks, workflows, and even business decisions autonomously.

item.name: AI value
item.description: The measurable business impact of AI investments — profitability, revenue, cost savings, productivity, innovation — which the report treats as the new benchmark for judging AI.
item.grounding_span: a small but consistent group of companies — the Pacesetters — outperform their peers across every measure of AI value.

item.name: Value beyond money
item.description: Non-financial returns from AI such as productivity, customer experience, and innovation, expected to become visible within 12 to 36 months.
item.grounding_span: Most companies expect the next 12 to 36 months to be the critical window where value beyond money becomes visible and transformative.

item.name: ROI from AI
item.description: Tangible returns on AI investment, which organizations face sharply rising urgency to demonstrate.
item.grounding_span: Eight in ten organizations say the urgency to demonstrate tangible ROI has risen sharply in the past six months, driven by CEOs, CFOs, IT leaders, and growing pressure from competitors.

item.name: AI use cases
item.description: Concrete applications of AI that organizations ideate, pilot, finalize, and move into production; progress through this lifecycle differentiates Pacesetters.
item.grounding_span: Progress in identifying practical AI use cases

item.name: AI monetization
item.description: Converting AI use cases into revenue; confidence in this remains uneven across organizations.
item.grounding_span: And confidence in monetizing AI isn't much better: 34% feel very confident, with 43% somewhat confident in monetizing their AI use cases.

item.name: AI impact measurement
item.description: Having a process and defined metrics to measure the impact of AI initiatives; a minority capability overall but near-universal among Pacesetters.
item.grounding_span: only one in three organizations (32%) have a process to measure the impact of their AI initiatives.

item.name: AI investment
item.description: Budget and spending directed at AI, accelerating even without supporting mechanisms like measurement and change management.
item.grounding_span: Ambition and investment in AI are also accelerating without important mechanisms to support the digital transformation underway.

item.name: Change management for AI adoption
item.description: Formal planning to guide employees through AI adoption; absent in most organizations and a risk to realizing ROI.
item.grounding_span: only one in three companies have a formal change management plan to guide employees through AI adoption.

item.name: AI workloads
item.description: Computational demand generated by AI systems, expected to grow rapidly and strain enterprise infrastructure, especially as agentic AI scales.
item.grounding_span: AI workloads are expected to grow rapidly over the next few years, creating significant demand on enterprise infrastructure.

item.grounding_span: Autonomous Software Engineering: AI agents that write, debug, test, and deploy code with minimal human input.

item.name: Cisco AI Readiness Index
item.method: Double-blind online survey (August 2025) of 8,039 senior business leaders in organizations with 500 or more employees across 30 markets and 26 industries; 49 weighted indicators across six pillars, scored by level of deployment and combined into an overall readiness score; analysis by independent third party Satori Experience.
item.grounding_span: The Cisco AI Readiness Index assesses organizations against six pillars of readiness—Strategy, Infrastructure, Data, Governance, Talent, and Culture—measured across 49 indicators.

item.text: Overall AI readiness score for each organization, calculated by combining weighted pillar scores.
item.grounding_span: Pillar scores were then combined to calculate an overall AI readiness score for each organization.

item.text: Level of deployment for each of the 49 readiness indicators.
item.response_type: deployment level: 25–50% for partial deployment, 100% for full deployment
item.grounding_span: Scores were assigned by level of deployment: 25–50% for partial deployment, 100% for full deployment.

item.grounding_span: The pillar weightings are as follows: Strategy (15%); Infrastructure (25%); Data (20%); Governance (15%); Talent (15%); and Culture (10%).

item.grounding_span: The Index measures AI readiness of companies across six pillars: Strategy, Infrastructure, Data, Governance, Talent, and Culture.

item.name: AI Readiness Levels (Pacesetters, Chasers, Followers, Laggards)
item.grounding_span: Based on their readiness scores, organizations are categorized into four levels:
AI Infrastructure Debt is the accumulation of gaps, trade-offs, short-cuts and lags in compute, networking, data management, security, and talent that compound as companies rush to deploy AI.
In the software world, this became known as technical debt: code written quickly to meet deadlines that eventually slows innovation.
the Pacesetters – the 13% of surveyed organizations globally that are fully ready for AI – who are capturing it.

Agentic AI systems don’t simply provide answers; as shown in the chart below, they take action by navigating tasks, workflows, and even business decisions autonomously.
Autonomous Software Engineering: AI agents that write, debug, test, and deploy code with minimal human input.
Personal and professional productivity agents: AI personal assistants that manage schedules, emails and task prioritization.
Industrial and Robotics Control Agents: Real-world agentic systems that control drones, autonomous vehicles, or warehouse robots.
Simulated Humans for Testing or Training: Autonomous agents in virtual environments for gaming, product testing, or user behavior simulation.
